nativeRange.habitat: Mesophotic (deep-reef) outer reef drop-offs at depths of 100–128 m off Rarotonga, where the species shelters secretively in interstices, cracks, and small caves along steep vertical walls.

diet.notes: The wild diet of the Narcosis Angelfish is essentially undocumented. In captivity, specimens are generally offered a varied diet that includes Spirulina, marine algae sheets, high-quality prepared angelfish foods, and frozen mysis or other small meaty items. Abundant live rock providing natural microalgae for grazing is considered important. Newly acquired individuals are often shy and may require targeted, low-competition feeding to ensure they are eating.

avoidWith: aggressive or boisterous tankmates that outcompete a reclusive deep-water specimen

summary: The Narcosis Angelfish (Centropyge narcosis) is an extremely rare pygmy angelfish endemic to Rarotonga, Cook Islands, collected from depths of 100 m or more. Its species name refers to the nitrogen narcosis its discoverers experienced during collection. A chilled, dimly-lit specialist aquarium is required.
careNotes: The Narcosis Angelfish (Centropyge narcosis) is a mesophotic, or deep-reef, species endemic to Rarotonga in the Cook Islands. It was originally collected from depths in excess of 100 m, and the species name 'narcosis' refers directly to the nitrogen narcosis the divers experienced during that discovery. Specimens appear in the aquarium trade only very rarely and command extreme prices when they do — the species is widely regarded as one for most aquarists to admire rather than pursue. Dim lighting, or at minimum a heavily shaded section of the tank, and a generously rockworked aquascape with caves, overhangs, and vertical drop-off features are considered essential. In the wild, the fish shelters in cracks along steep walls and adopts an inverted, head-down posture much of the time, so replicating that environment is important for settling a specimen in captivity. The species is generally considered reef-safe with caution: trade reports describe a tendency to nip at fleshy large-polyp stony corals (LPS), including disc corals such as Fungia and Cycloseris and chalice corals in the genera Echinophyllia, Echinopora, and Mycedium. A reef aquarium dominated by small-polyp stony corals (SPS) tends to be a safer placement than a mixed display with significant LPS coverage. Like other Centropyge angelfish, this species is a protogynous hermaphrodite — individuals are born female and the dominant animal in a group may change sex to become male. Small haremic groups form on Rarotongan deep reefs, but specimens are so scarce in the trade that captive harems are essentially unheard of. Decompression injury during collection is a significant source of early mortality, and surviving specimens may carry lasting effects, so any aquarist taking on this species should expect specialist-level acclimatisation and quarantine demands.
breedingNotes: No captive breeding has been documented. In the wild, the Narcosis Angelfish has been reported to form small haremic groups on the deep reefs off Rarotonga, and like other Centropyge it is a protogynous hermaphrodite in which the dominant female can transition to male.

primary.name: FishBase
primary.notes: Used as primary because LiveAquaria product page (https://www.liveaquaria.com/products/narcosis-angelfish and ...-expert-only variant) returned 404 — the species is essentially never stocked. FishBase confirmed taxonomy (Pomacanthidae, Acanthuriformes), max length 5.5 cm SL, depth range 100–128 m, endemic to Rarotonga, Cook Islands.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Centropyge narcosis
additional.notes: Cross-checked: family/order, max length 5.5 cm, Cook Islands endemism, depth 100–128 m, Pyle & Randall (1993) description, etymology ('nitrogen narcosis' suffered during collection), behavioural notes (pairs/small groups, inverted swimming posture, defensive head-down with erected dorsal spines), aquarium trade scarcity.

additional.name: Reeflex
additional.notes: Provided husbandry-style figures: minimum tank ~450 L, adult size to 8 cm TL, temperature range 25–28 °C, diet (algae + meaty frozen). Disagrees with FishBase on max size (5.5 cm SL vs 8 cm TL — averaged to a 5.5–8 cm range) and with trade write-ups on temperature (Reeflex 25–28 °C vs trade/aquarist 22–23 °C / chiller-required — averaged toward the cooler end given the species' mesophotic collection depth).

additional.name: Tropical Fish Keeping — Narcosis Angelfish
additional.notes: Cross-checked husbandry prose: 55–70 gal (208–265 L) deepwater reef recommended by retailers, chiller required to keep maximum tank temperature below ~74 °F (23 °C), dimmed lighting, deep-reef aquascape with caves/overhangs, generally reef-safe with caution (nips Fungia/Cycloseris and chalice corals — Echinophyllia, Echinopora, Mycedium — but infrequent on healthy specimens when well fed). Tank size biased upward to 300 L per playbook (retailers under-state, species is shy/grail-class).

fish.breedingNotes: Not bred in captivity. In the wild, this species forms small haremic groups and is a protogynous hermaphrodite in which the dominant female transitions to male.

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: Deepwater (100+ m) Cook Islands endemic; almost never enters the trade, commands extreme prices, and requires a chilled (~22–24 °C), dimly-lit specialist setup with decompression-aware acclimation. Inappropriate for any non-specialist setup.
